items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: Who developed the symphonic poem as a one-movement orchestral form?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Liszt. “Berlioz” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is music without an extra-musical narrative called?
items.why: Correct answer: Absolute music. “Programme music” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Berlioz premiere his programmatic 'Symphonie fantastique'?
items.why: The real figure is 1830. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
